---
title: "Bioinformatics for Environmental Sciences"
subtitle: "Syllabus — FISH 546 (2026)"
---

## About

This course teaches graduate students how to move from biological sequence data to defensible environmental bioinformatics interpretation. Each student develops and completes a research project targeting a reproducible final compendium by the end of the quarter. The weekly topics emphasize bioinformatics data types and analyses; computing tools such as RStudio, Quarto, GitHub, Raven, Hyak, and archiving are handled through [self-directed support modules](support.qmd) and used throughout the quarter.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-important}
## Do this before Week 1
Complete the **three self-directed setup tutorials** on the [Before Class](setup.qmd) page — one for each computing platform you will use (your own computer, Raven, and Hyak). Then open your **Setup Check** issue. You cannot keep up in Week 1 without a working setup on all three.
:::

Bioinformatics is not only running tools. It is the practice of keeping data provenance clear, choosing appropriate analyses, checking intermediate products, and making biological claims that the evidence can support.

## Format

This class is driven in part by student needs and is flexible in content. It is practical and problem-driven. Tuesdays focus on the week's bioinformatics concept and data type. Thursdays are working sessions for applying that concept to assignments and research projects.

::: {.callout-important style="color: orange"}
**Issues are how we work.** Structured, trackable work happens in **GitHub Issues** using course [issue forms](https://github.com/course-fish546-2026) — weekly progress, project proposals, and help requests. Open-ended conversation happens in [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/orgs/course-fish546-2026/discussions) and on [Slack](https://genefish.slack.com). Be sure you have a GitHub account, are part of the course organization, and have notifications turned on.
:::

## Platforms

You will work across three computing tiers. Each has a dedicated [setup tutorial](setup.qmd), and the operational details live on the [Self-Directed Skills](support.qmd) page.

| Tier | Platform | Access |
|------|----------|--------|
| Your computer | Local shell + Git/GitHub | your laptop/desktop |
| Raven | RStudio Server | <http://raven.fish.washington.edu:8787> |
| Hyak / Klone | HPC (SLURM + containers) | `ssh <UWNetID>@klone.hyak.uw.edu` · [On-Demand](https://ondemand.hyak.uw.edu) |

Course GitHub Organization: <https://github.com/course-fish546-2026>

## Textbook

[Bioinformatics Data Skills](https://www.amazon.com/Bioinformatics-Data-Skills-Reproducible-Research/dp/1449367372/) — Reproducible and Robust Research with Open Source Tools, by Vince Buffalo (O'Reilly, 2015). [Supplementary repo](https://github.com/vsbuffalo/bds-files).

## Grading

Each week has a bioinformatics assignment, expected research-project progress, and a question set. You will give a mid-quarter project update and a final compendium presentation. Question sets are based on the week's reading/material and are due by 3:00pm Tuesday - review the [weekly topic](schedule.qmd) before class.

| *Assignments* | Grade % | *comment* |
|----|----|----|
| Weekly Question Set | 25% | due Tuesday 3:00pm |
| Weekly Class Assignment | 35% | due Friday 5:00pm |
| Weekly Research Project Progress | 20% | assessed via your **weekly-progress issue** + project board |
| Project Presentation | 10% | mid-quarter update + final presentation |
| Project Completion | 10% | Week 10: compendium |

## Submitting work

- **Question sets** → files in your repo's `homework/` directory, submitted with a question-set submission issue that points to a commit SHA and manifest.
- **Class assignments** → files in your repo's `assignment/` directory, submitted with an assignment submission issue that points to a commit SHA and manifest.
- **Project progress** → assessed from your **weekly-progress GitHub issue** and your card on the course project board (goals set vs. met, issues resolved, best practices applied).

See [Turn In Work](turn-in.qmd) for the submission contract and automatic assessment report format.

**Late policy:** 40% deduction; not accepted more than 5 days past due.

## Core competencies

Skills in this course are anchored to the **[Roberts Lab Handbook](https://robertslab.github.io/resources/)** — its computing best practices, data management, and platform guides (Raven, Klone) are the reference standard for how you are expected to work.

## Weekly arc

The course moves from sequence identification and raw sequencing data toward multi-assay environmental interpretation:

1. Sequence databases and BLAST.
2. Raw reads, FASTQ, quality control, and trimming.
3. Mapping, alignment, assembly, and quantification.
4. RNA-seq and differential expression.
5. Genome annotation and genomic ranges.
6. Variants and population signals.
7. DNA methylation and regulatory genomics.
8. eDNA and metabarcoding.
9. Project synthesis and biological interpretation.
10. Final compendium.

## Useful resources

- [Roberts Lab Handbook](https://robertslab.github.io/resources/) — computing, data management, and platform guides.
- [MarineOmics Portal](https://marineomics.github.io) — genomic tutorials.
- [sandbox.bio](https://sandbox.bio) — interactive bioinformatics tutorials.
- [Happy Git with R](https://happygitwithr.com/) — Git + GitHub + RStudio.
